county_metadata.record_losses: Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records lost
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1833 from Wayne. County seat: Doniphan. Ozark Highlands region with rugged terrain and Southern Appalachian settlement. Burned county: Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records lost
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Doniphan holds records restarting after Civil War courthouse destruction. For pre-war records, check Wayne County (parent), Missouri State Archives, and BLM GLO land patents. Ripley County is in the southeastern Ozarks on the Current River. Severe Civil War guerrilla warfare devastated the area. Ripley County Historical Society in Doniphan has post-war genealogical files. Strong Southern Appalachian settlement heritage. Current River was a timber floating route. FamilySearch microfilm covers post-war records. Church records are essential supplements.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO original land patents; county Recorder of Deeds records from 1833

source_profiles.source_name: Ripley County Deed Records -- Recorder of Deeds, Doniphan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1833 to present. Post-destruction records; pre-1833 records largely lost.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records lost

source_profiles.source_name: Ripley County Probate Records -- Probate Court, Doniphan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, administrations, and guardianship records from 1833. Missouri probate courts also handle some adoption records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records lost

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records from 1833. Missouri marriage records kept at county level by Recorder of Deeds. FamilySearch has indexed many Missouri marriages.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records lost

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1820 from Cooper. County seat: Marshall. Historic Boonslick region — earliest area of American settlement west of St. Louis. Part of Missouri's 'Little Dixie' — strong Southern heritage and slaveholding region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Marshall holds land records from 1820. Saline County is in the 'Little Dixie' heartland — one of Missouri's most prominent slaveholding counties with extensive enslaved persons records in probate, tax, and estate records. Marshall was a significant community in the hemp and tobacco economy. Missouri Valley College in Marshall has local history materials. Saline County Historical Society maintains genealogical files. Strong Virginia and Kentucky settlement heritage. Battle of Marshall (1863). FamilySearch microfilm is extensive. Missouri State Archives holds circuit court records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO original land patents; county Recorder of Deeds records from 1820

source_profiles.source_name: Saline County Deed Records -- Recorder of Deeds, Marshall
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1820 to present. Continuous coverage from county formation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation (1820)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, administrations, and guardianship records from 1820. Missouri probate courts also handle some adoption records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1845 from Adair. County seat: Lancaster.
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Lancaster holds land records from 1845. Schuyler County is one of Missouri's smallest counties in northeast Missouri near the Iowa border. Schuyler County Historical Society has genealogical files. Check Adair County for pre-1845 records. Small agricultural community — church records (Baptist, Methodist) are essential supplements. FamilySearch microfilm covers deed books and probate. Missouri State Archives holds circuit court records. BLM GLO records document original land entries.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1841 from Clark, Lewis, Shelby. County seat: Memphis.
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Memphis holds land records from 1841. Scotland County is in northeast Missouri. Scotland County Historical Society in Memphis has genealogical files. The county was named for Scotland by settlers of Scottish heritage. Strong Scottish and Scots-Irish settlement. Check Clark, Lewis, and Shelby counties for pre-1841 records. FamilySearch microfilm covers deed books and probate. Missouri State Archives holds circuit court records. Presbyterian church records are particularly important in this county.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1821 from New Madrid. County seat: Benton. Missouri Bootheel (southeastern lowlands) — cotton agriculture region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Benton holds land records from 1821. Scott County is in the Bootheel region with Sikeston and other communities. Significant African American population — sharecropping and tenant farming records. Sikeston Heritage Museum. Check New Madrid County for pre-1821 records. Spanish colonial records relate to this area through the New Madrid district. Cotton and soybean agriculture. Scott County Historical Society has genealogical files. FamilySearch microfilm covers deed books and probate. Missouri State Archives holds circuit court records.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1863 courthouse burned during Civil War; 1938 courthouse fire destroyed additional records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1841 from Ripley. County seat: Eminence. Ozark Highlands region with rugged terrain and Southern Appalachian settlement. Burned county: 1863 courthouse burned during Civil War; 1938 courthouse fire destroyed additional records
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Eminence holds records restarting after the 1863 Civil War courthouse burning and the 1938 fire that destroyed additional records. For pre-fire research, check Ripley County (parent), Missouri State Archives, and BLM GLO land patents. Shannon County is one of the most remote and sparsely populated counties in Missouri. Ozark National Scenic Riverways (Current and Jacks Fork Rivers). Strong Southern Appalachian settlement heritage. Shannon County Historical Society in Eminence has limited post-fire files. FamilySearch microfilm covers available records. Timber industry records supplement county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO original land patents; county Recorder of Deeds records from 1841

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1835 from Marion. County seat: Shelbyville.
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Shelbyville holds land records from 1835. Shelby County Historical Society in Shelbyville has genealogical files. Northeast Missouri agricultural county. Clarence, Hunnewell, and Shelbina are other communities. Salt River valley settlement. Check Marion County for pre-1835 records. Strong Southern settlement from Kentucky and Virginia. FamilySearch microfilm covers deed books and probate. Missouri State Archives holds circuit court records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1812 from Original district. County seat: St. Charles. French colonial heritage — area settled under French and Spanish governance before Louisiana Purchase (1803).
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in St. Charles holds land records from 1812 (original district). St. Charles was Missouri's first state capital (1821-1826). French colonial heritage — St. Charles was founded as 'Les Petites Cotes' in 1769. Lewis and Clark embarked from St. Charles in 1804. St. Charles County Historical Society has exceptional genealogical collections. The county has both French colonial and early American settlement records. Daniel Boone's homestead was here. St. Charles Borromeo Catholic Church records from the 1790s. Spanish colonial land grants at Missouri State Archives. Significant German immigration in the 1830s-1860s. FamilySearch microfilm is extensive for this well-documented county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Missouri State Archives microfilm and digitized records for St. Charles from colonial era

source_profiles.repository: Missouri State Archives, Jefferson City / Ste. Genevieve Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: French colonial court records, Spanish colonial land grants, parish registers, and administrative records for the St. Charles area. Louisiana Purchase (1803) transferred governance to the US.

source_profiles.repository: Local congregations / Concordia Historical Institute / Missouri State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: German Evangelical, Lutheran, Catholic, and Reformed church records for St. Charles. Baptisms, marriages, burials, and confirmations. Many records in German language. Concordia Historical Institute (Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod) in St. Louis holds synod records.

county_metadata.record_losses: Order No. 11 (1863) and Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records destroyed
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1841 from Rives (renamed Henry). County seat: Osceola. Affected by Order No. 11 (1863) — forced depopulation of western Missouri border counties during Civil War. Burned county: Order No. 11 (1863) and Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; most early records destroyed
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Osceola holds records restarting after Order No. 11 (1863) and Civil War destruction. The courthouse was burned and most early records destroyed. For pre-1863 records, check Rives/Henry County (parent), Missouri State Archives, and BLM GLO records. Osceola was raided and burned by Jayhawker Jim Lane in 1861, one of the most notorious incidents of the border war. St. Clair County Historical Society in Osceola has post-war genealogical files. Harry S Truman Lake now covers former settlement areas. FamilySearch microfilm covers post-war records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO original land patents; county Recorder of Deeds records from 1841

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Missouri county formed 1821 from Jefferson, Ste. Genevieve, Washington. County seat: Farmington. Missouri Lead Mining District — lead mining from French colonial era to present.
county_metadata.research_tips: Recorder of Deeds in Farmington holds land records from 1821. St. Francois County is in the heart of Missouri's Old Lead Belt — lead mining has been continuous since French colonial times. Bonne Terre Mine is now a tourist attraction. St. Francois County Historical Society in Farmington has genealogical files. Significant French colonial heritage. Check Jefferson, Ste. Genevieve, and Washington counties for pre-1821 records. Strong German and Cornish miner immigration. Catholic parish records from early settlement. FamilySearch microfilm covers deed books and probate. Missouri Mines State Historic Site in Park Hills has mining company records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO original land patents; county Recorder of Deeds records from 1821
